clusters.similarity_summary: Historical women-in-service protagonists fall into forbidden same-sex attachments while doing recurring ritual labor meant to mediate the dead, so the novel engine is intimacy-through-devotional work rather than exterior plot.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: A subordinate woman is drawn deeper into a closed household or religious operation where repeated care work around dead bodies or messages turns partnership into faith and desire.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Material dependence or vows keep the lovers inside the system even as grief, doctrine, and social prohibition make the attachment ruinous.
clusters.shared_world_shape: An older historical institution treats contact with the dead as practical labor, with seasons, hierarchy, and bodily ritual shaping daily life.

clusters.similarity_summary: Working-class ensembles turn semi-divine animal or carcass matter into saleable goods, and the books keep generating harvest, concealment, barter, and rivalry scenes as the sacred resource starts misbehaving.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: A crew or kin group monetizes a mythic body's byproducts through dirty extraction and distribution work.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Scarcity, private schemes, and uncanny side effects escalate until profit-seeking threatens the group and the local social order.
clusters.shared_world_shape: The setting treats divine or legendary biology as materially useful infrastructure, so holiness is inseparable from mud, hauling, butchery, and black markets.

clusters.similarity_summary: Women inside wartime shelter or transport systems become brokers of performative identity, deciding who can pass into safety under military threat.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: A female insider learns the codes of an embattled institution and uses names, gestures, or respectability scripts to move vulnerable people through it.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Imminent violence compresses every decision into triage while forbidden desire and family obligation distort who gets protected.
clusters.shared_world_shape: Safety depends on legibility inside a hierarchical wartime refuge structure, with gates, berths, and assigned statuses determining survival.

clusters.similarity_summary: Long-span civic ritual operators become the memory organs of a town as the same public ceremonies recur across decades.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: A foul-mouthed, indispensable worker repeatedly stages or executes a communal rite that keeps revealing the town to itself.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Aging, scandal, weather, politics, and family resentment keep changing what the ritual means without allowing escape from it.
clusters.shared_world_shape: Public life is organized around recurring civic or saintly ceremonies whose procedural details anchor decades of local history.

clusters.similarity_summary: This is a concealment-driven estuary labor triangle rather than a public institution or naming-system duplicate.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: Three oyster farmers keep working the reef after a killing, so everyday shellfish labor becomes the vessel that preserves and corrodes their shared lie.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Storm seasons, ecological decline, and erotic rivalry keep the secret active for years instead of resolving it quickly.
clusters.shared_world_shape: A sinking working estuary makes guilt inseparable from tidal craft, poisoned water, and survival economics.

clusters.similarity_summary: Status and kinship are materially installed into bodies through artisanal name or flesh work, making these all workshop or procedural novels about manufacturing legibility.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: A lower-status craftsperson or crew performs stepwise bodily modifications that let clients pass into marriage, class, or civic standing.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Debt and forbidden attachment make the maker both dependent on and excluded from the elevated lives the work creates.
clusters.shared_world_shape: Identity is a bodily technology governed by rituals of fitting, grafting, rehearsal, and public display rather than abstract paperwork.

clusters.similarity_summary: These are route novels about moving through disaster landscapes while managing belief economies around possibly real miracles or gods.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: A mobile crew or family carries a sacred performance, body, or scam from stop to stop, with each town producing new believers, hustles, and logistical mess.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Floods, evacuations, succession fights, or public panic force the travelers to keep the show on the road even as authenticity becomes unstable.
clusters.shared_world_shape: A damaged regional landscape relies on itinerant miracle traffic, so spirituality is inseparable from buses, caravans, camps, and roadside labor.

clusters.similarity_summary: Decades-long obsession novels where a craft professional secretly keeps remaking the same impossible object in hopes of undoing family loss.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: A worker folds private fixation into a respectable trade, using each new commission or city as another attempt to perfect the missing thing.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Time, money, theft, and family decay deepen the compulsion instead of curing it.
clusters.shared_world_shape: History moves through commerce and craft circulation, letting obsession masquerade as ordinary professional life for years.

clusters.similarity_summary: Aquatic biotech labor novels where community survival depends on altered marine life and the workers' own bodies start blurring with the system that employs them.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: A caretaker or young laborer keeps a biologically engineered aquatic economy running through repetitive husbandry, diving, triage, or clinical maintenance.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Disease, contamination, and desire destabilize which bodies are usable, loved, or still human enough to belong.
clusters.shared_world_shape: The setting ties food, power, afterlife, or wages to modified marine organisms, making ecology and body management the same daily problem.

clusters.similarity_summary: Accidental public authorities over unstable naming systems become central once language stops attaching cleanly to bodies or products.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: A language worker's ordinary job turns into adjudicating reality when names begin producing or misidentifying the things they label.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Grief, commerce, rumor, and public demand make every naming decision socially explosive.
clusters.shared_world_shape: Modern or proto-modern cities run on linguistic legibility, so a breakdown in naming becomes a civic event rather than a private metaphor.

clusters.similarity_summary: A bodily laborer weaponizes a relic spectacle to fight an elite land claim, which is distinct from the long-span civic-ritual books and the intimate devotional romances.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: An older working woman turns the very fertility ritual economy that exploits her into a counter-performance against property power.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Storm exposure, class humiliation, and the race to control a saint's remains drive the conflict.
clusters.shared_world_shape: Marsh access, fertility commerce, and local sanctity are fused, so land ownership is contested through bodies, mud, and public piety.

clusters.similarity_summary: Plural civic-emergency novels where public safety infrastructure only works through contested devotional performance.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: An ensemble of neighborhood operators, believers, and rivals tries to keep a crisis-response system functioning while factions weaponize it.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Imminent catastrophe, retaliatory violence, and disputed signs force collective decisions before trust collapses.
clusters.shared_world_shape: City infrastructure is inseparable from choir, prayer, ritual, or sacred sound, so technical failure immediately becomes social and spiritual conflict.

clusters.similarity_summary: Domestic-fixer comedies about marginalized women quietly keeping households livable by manipulating the sentient systems that judge or bless home life.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: A repairwoman or improvised household manager services semi-sentient domestic entities so ordinary people can keep shelter, status, and intimacy intact.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Debt and reform pressure threaten to expose all the informal maintenance that makes private life possible.
clusters.shared_world_shape: Homes are active social regulators, whether as scored devices or minor gods, and domestic upkeep doubles as covert welfare infrastructure.

clusters.similarity_summary: This is an inheritance-murder return to a war-made mountain fortress, structurally distinct from the other institutional or naming books.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: A displaced daughter comes home to reconstruct a death and an older campaign's competing memories inside family private law.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Succession panic and mutually incompatible testimonies keep every kin interaction adversarial.
clusters.shared_world_shape: Border claims, ceremonial hunts, and ancestral fort rules shape politics more than modern bureaucracy.

clusters.similarity_summary: This is a refugee-burial convoy novel driven by kin obligation and contested resting place, not by miracle commerce or identity fabrication.
clusters.shared_engine_shape: A mother has to move living relatives and ancestral remains together through an evacuation route that keeps testing family cohesion.
clusters.shared_pressure_shape: Storm season, armed hostility, revenge impulses, and burial demands all pull the convoy toward rupture.
clusters.shared_world_shape: Belonging is geographic and funerary: who can travel, settle, or bury their dead determines survival.
